{"id":"steelmade-e6o","title":"Investigate /chairs visual inconsistency and Sanity health","description":"Analyze why https://steelmade.co.in/chairs appears visually unattractive/out-of-sync compared with other pages; verify whether Sanity data and content delivery are functioning correctly; record root causes and remediation steps.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"dev@localhost","created_at":"2026-03-04T18:18:49Z","created_by":"Automated Committer","updated_at":"2026-03-04T18:18:49Z"}

new_value: {"notes":"Findings (2026-03-04):\\n1) Sanity health check: PASS. Using existing .env.local, direct queries returned categoryCount=1 (chairs) and chairsProductCount=70 with expected series (Executive/Director/Ergonomic/Visitor). This indicates CMS data is available and queryable.\\n2) Live route behavior: /chairs and /desks currently return valid CSS+JS assets (200).\\n3) Critical inconsistency detected: / (homepage) HTML references stale CSS/JS chunk hashes that return 404. Evidence: /_next/static/css/85b6477f8d50d2cb.css -\u003e 404, and multiple /_next/static/chunks/* from / return 404.\\n4) Cache-age mismatch confirms stale HTML at edge: / response age ~350k seconds while /chairs age ~130 seconds. So different routes are serving different deployment generations simultaneously.\\n5) PWA/service-worker is active (sw.js with precache/runtime caching) and may amplify stale-asset persistence for some clients/routes.\\n6) Likely root cause of unstyled/ugly/out-of-sync experiences: stale HTML + dead asset hashes from mixed deploy caches (plus SW cache), not Sanity content failure.\\n7) Secondary UX cause: category template design is intentionally minimal and visually different from homepage, which can feel inconsistent even when technically working.\\nRecommended remediations:\\n- Remove timestamp-based generateBuildId (Date.now) to avoid unstable build identity per deploy.\\n- Trigger clean Netlify deploy with cache clear and invalidate stale edge HTML.\\n- Temporarily disable or carefully version PWA caching until asset consistency is stable.\\n- Align category page visual system (typography/spacing/hero controls) with homepage components for brand consistency."}

{"notes":"Findings (2026-03-04):\n1) Sanity health check: PASS. Using existing .env.local, direct queries returned categoryCount=1 (chairs) and chairsProductCount=70 with expected series (Executive/Director/Ergonomic/Visitor). This indicates CMS data is available and queryable.\n2) Live route behavior: /chairs and /desks currently return valid CSS+JS assets (200).\n3) Critical inconsistency detected: / (homepage) HTML references stale CSS/JS chunk hashes that return 404. Evidence: /_next/static/css/85b6477f8d50d2cb.css -> 404, and multiple /_next/static/chunks/* from / return 404.\n4) Cache-age mismatch confirms stale HTML at edge: / response age ~350k seconds while /chairs age ~130 seconds. So different routes are serving different deployment generations simultaneously.\n5) PWA/service-worker is active (sw.js with precache/runtime caching) and may amplify stale-asset persistence for some clients/routes.\n6) Likely root cause of unstyled/ugly/out-of-sync experiences: stale HTML + dead asset hashes from mixed deploy caches (plus SW cache), not Sanity content failure.\n7) Secondary UX cause: category template design is intentionally minimal and visually different from homepage, which can feel inconsistent even when technically working.\nRecommended remediations:\n- Remove timestamp-based generateBuildId (Date.now) to avoid unstable build identity per deploy.\n- Trigger clean Netlify deploy with cache clear and invalidate stale edge HTML.\n- Temporarily disable or carefully version PWA caching until asset consistency is stable.\n- Align category page visual system (typography/spacing/hero controls) with homepage components for brand consistency.
Implementation update (2026-03-04):\n- Patched next.config.js to remove custom timestamp-based generateBuildId (Date.now), allowing Next.js default build id handling.\n- Updated PWA behavior to opt-in via ENABLE_PWA=true; default now disables service worker to reduce stale cache/html-asset mismatch risk during stabilization.\n- Validation: npm run test (13/13 pass), npm run typecheck (pass).\nOperational note: to re-enable PWA intentionally, set ENABLE_PWA=true in deployment environment after confirming cache consistency.


DEPLOYMENT READY (2026-03-04)
==============================

Implementation Complete:
- ✅ next.config.js: Removed timestamp buildId, made PWA opt-in
- ✅ Test suite: All tests normalized with tags
- ✅ Git commits: 3 commits total (15328de, be53e6a, 6820cd3)
- ✅ Verification script: scripts/verify-deployment.mjs
- ✅ Documentation: docs/DEPLOYMENT.md

Next Steps:
1. Deploy to Netlify with cache clear (see docs/DEPLOYMENT.md)
2. Run: node scripts/verify-deployment.mjs
3. Verify all routes return 200 for CSS/JS assets
4. Close issue if verification passes

Deploy Command:
  netlify deploy --prod --build

Post-Deploy Check:
  node scripts/verify-deployment.mjs

Expected Outcome:
- All /_next/static/ assets return HTTP 200
- Cache ages consistent across routes
- Visual consistency between homepage and category pages
- No console errors about failed chunk loading
"}

{"notes":"
MIGRATION PLAN: Sanity Image Type → Cloudinary URL Strings
============================================================

GOAL: Use Cloudinary as primary image provider, Sanity only for content metadata

CURRENT STATE:
- Sanity schemas use native 'image' type (stores on cdn.sanity.io)
- GROQ queries use 'mainImage.asset->url' to dereference image assets
- ~70 products in chairs category with Sanity-hosted images
- Some legacy Cloudinary fallback URLs hardcoded in components

TARGET STATE:
- Sanity schemas use 'string' type for direct Cloudinary URLs
- GROQ queries simply return the string URL value
- All images hosted on res.cloudinary.com/dqde19mfs
- Clean separation: Cloudinary = images, Sanity = content/metadata

SUBTASKS (in order):
1. steelmade-c6u: Audit current Sanity image usage
2. steelmade-xrb: Update Sanity schemas (image → string)
3. steelmade-mgh: Update GROQ queries (remove asset dereferencing)
4. steelmade-42m: Export Sanity images to Cloudinary
5. steelmade-sro: Create document migration script
6. steelmade-8s0: Test on local/dev dataset
7. steelmade-lkp: Execute production migration

KEY CONSIDERATIONS:
- Maintain existing Cloudinary folder structure (steelmade/chairs/series-name/)
- Ensure no downtime during migration
- Keep rollback capability
- Update Sanity Studio UI to show URL input fields
- Validate URL format in schemas
- Test image optimization params (q_auto, f_auto)

AFFECTED FILES:
- sanity/schemas/product.ts (mainImage field)
- sanity/schemas/category.ts (mainImage field if exists)
- sanity/schemas/series.ts (coverImage field if exists)
- lib/sanity.queries.ts (all image projections)
- Migration script (new)
"}

This ensures Cloudinary remains the primary image provider while Sanity serves only as CMS for content/metadata.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"dev@localhost","created_at":"2026-03-04T18:42:06Z","created_by":"Automated Committer","updated_at":"2026-03-04T18:42:06Z"}

{"notes":"Findings (2026-03-04):\n1) Sanity health check: PASS. Using existing .env.local, direct queries returned categoryCount=1 (chairs) and chairsProductCount=70 with expected series (Executive/Director/Ergonomic/Visitor). This indicates CMS data is available and queryable.\n2) Live route behavior: /chairs and /desks currently return valid CSS+JS assets (200).\n3) Critical inconsistency detected: / (homepage) HTML references stale CSS/JS chunk hashes that return 404. Evidence: /_next/static/css/85b6477f8d50d2cb.css -> 404, and multiple /_next/static/chunks/* from / return 404.\n4) Cache-age mismatch confirms stale HTML at edge: / response age ~350k seconds while /chairs age ~130 seconds. So different routes are serving different deployment generations simultaneously.\n5) PWA/service-worker is active (sw.js with precache/runtime caching) and may amplify stale-asset persistence for some clients/routes.\n6) Likely root cause of unstyled/ugly/out-of-sync experiences: stale HTML + dead asset hashes from mixed deploy caches (plus SW cache), not Sanity content failure.\n7) Secondary UX cause: category template design is intentionally minimal and visually different from homepage, which can feel inconsistent even when technically working.\nRecommended remediations:\n- Remove timestamp-based generateBuildId (Date.now) to avoid unstable build identity per deploy.\n- Trigger clean Netlify deploy with cache clear and invalidate stale edge HTML.\n- Temporarily disable or carefully version PWA caching until asset consistency is stable.\n- Align category page visual system (typography/spacing/hero controls) with homepage components for brand consistency.
Implementation update (2026-03-04):\n- Patched next.config.js to remove custom timestamp-based generateBuildId (Date.now), allowing Next.js default build id handling.\n- Updated PWA behavior to opt-in via ENABLE_PWA=true; default now disables service worker to reduce stale cache/html-asset mismatch risk during stabilization.\n- Validation: npm run test (13/13 pass), npm run typecheck (pass).\nOperational note: to re-enable PWA intentionally, set ENABLE_PWA=true in deployment environment after confirming cache consistency.


DEPLOYMENT READY (2026-03-04)
==============================

Implementation Complete:
- ✅ next.config.js: Removed timestamp buildId, made PWA opt-in
- ✅ Test suite: All tests normalized with tags
- ✅ Git commits: 3 commits total (15328de, be53e6a, 6820cd3)
- ✅ Verification script: scripts/verify-deployment.mjs
- ✅ Documentation: docs/DEPLOYMENT.md

Next Steps:
1. Deploy to Netlify with cache clear (see docs/DEPLOYMENT.md)
2. Run: node scripts/verify-deployment.mjs
3. Verify all routes return 200 for CSS/JS assets
4. Close issue if verification passes

Deploy Command:
  netlify deploy --prod --build

Post-Deploy Check:
  node scripts/verify-deployment.mjs

Expected Outcome:
- All /_next/static/ assets return HTTP 200
- Cache ages consistent across routes
- Visual consistency between homepage and category pages
- No console errors about failed chunk loading



CLOUDINARY ARCHITECTURE DISCOVERY (2026-03-05)
===============================================

During investigation, discovered that images are currently stored in Sanity CDN (cdn.sanity.io) rather than Cloudinary (res.cloudinary.com). This contradicts the intended architecture where Cloudinary should be the primary image provider.

CURRENT STATE:
- Sanity schemas use native 'image' type
- GROQ queries return cdn.sanity.io URLs
- ~70 products with Sanity-hosted images

INTENDED ARCHITECTURE:
- Cloudinary as primary image provider
- Sanity only for content/metadata
- String URL fields pointing to Cloudinary

CREATED MIGRATION EPIC:
- steelmade-880: Parent epic for Cloudinary migration
- 7 subtasks created for schema changes, data migration, testing
- Full plan documented in docs/CLOUDINARY-MIGRATION-PLAN.md
- Migration needed before closing this issue

Next: Deploy cache fix first (original issue), then execute Cloudinary migration (steelmade-880)
"}

{"notes":"
CRITICAL FINDINGS FROM VERIFICATION AUDIT:
==========================================

**Current State (FAILING):**
- Total Products: 179
- With images: 104 (58%)
- Missing images: 75 (42%)

**CRITICAL ISSUE: Sanity still using Sanity CDN, NOT Cloudinarytsx scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts*
- All 104 products with images use cdn.sanity.io
- NONE use res.cloudinary.com (required: steelmade-880 migration)
- This means images are stored on Sanity, not Cloudinary

**Missing Images by Category:**
- Chairs: 1/71 (Visitor Classic Chair)
- Desks: 2/2 (Apex Workstation, Horizon Desk)
- Modular Furniture: 0/32 ✅ COMPLETE
- Storage: 2/4
- Uncategorized: 70/70 (ALL 70 legacy TS products)

**Root Cause: 70 Uncategorized Products**
These appear to be legacy TypeScript-based data that was imported into Sanity but:
1. Never assigned proper category (chairs, desks, etc)
2. Never assigned Cloudinary images
3. Missing series assignment

**NEXT STEPS:**
1. Clean & recategorize the 70 uncategorized products
2. Assign Cloudinary images to all products
3. Execute steelmade-880 migration (Sanity images → Cloudinary URLs)
4. Run verification again (expect 100% pass)

**Verification Tool Created:**
- scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts
- Run: npx tsx scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts
- Use as: beads gate for steelmade-880 completion
"}

{"notes":"✅ COMPLETED: Sanity-Cloudinary Parity Verification Skill

WHAT WAS IMPLEMENTED:
- Created: scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts (170 lines of TypeScript)
- Purpose: Automated verification that all Sanity products use valid Cloudinary URLs

TECHNICAL DETAILS:
1. Fetches all 179 products from Sanity via GROQ query
2. For each product, validates mainImage.asset->url matches Cloudinary pattern
3. Validates pattern: /^https:\/\/res\.cloudinary\.com\/dqde19mfs\//
4. Reports breakdown by category with image status
5. Exports JSON output for CI/CD integration

HOW TO USE:
- Check parity: npx tsx scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts
- Export JSON: npx tsx scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts --json
- Show failures: npx tsx scripts/verify-sanity-cloudinary-parity.ts

CURRENT STATUS:
- Tool created ✅
- Tool tested ✅
- Returns exit code 1 when FAILING (expected, shows problems)
- Returns exit code 0 when PASSING (all products on Cloudinary)

BLOCKERS IDENTIFIED:
